outcome_contract.why: Important details about each email thread -- like the guest's name, their booking reference, and room number -- are all crammed into one text field. Every time the system needs any of these details, it has to unpack the entire blob. This is slow, error-prone, and makes it impossible to search or filter threads by guest name or booking reference directly. Moving the most-used fields into proper database columns would make the inbox faster and more reliable.
outcome_contract.intended_outcome_statement: A validated next action exists for metadata normalization, with target fields identified, migration strategy defined, and backward compatibility approach documented.

engineering_coverage.applicable: N/A -- no UI changes in either option
PII fields (guest name, booking ref) become named columns; same auth boundary; no new exposure
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: PII handling unchanged; column names make fields more discoverable in schema but not in API

engineering_coverage.applicable: Can add migration-complete logging per tier
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Log backfill row count and any errors

engineering_coverage.applicable: Test fixtures updated per tier; smaller diffs per PR; can validate each tier independently
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: All test fixtures updated at once; single validation pass
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Option B: single validation pass is simpler but harder to review. Option A: incremental validation but more test churn. Difference is marginal given modest test surface.

engineering_coverage.applicable: Dual-write keeps metadata_json populated; API `metadata` response reconstituted from columns; type unification can happen per tier
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Type unification happens once; metadata_json kept as frozen JSON backup; API `metadata` response built from columns
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Must keep `metadata` API response field populated regardless of option. In both cases, `parseThreadMetadata` becomes a column-to-object mapper.

engineering_coverage.applicable: Incremental improvement per tier; `needsManualDraft` indexed first
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: All fields available as columns immediately; all json_extract and JSON.parse eliminated at once
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Performance gain is marginal given modest row count. Main win is code clarity, not speed.

engineering_coverage.applicable: Tier rollback: deploy previous code, new columns ignored. Very safe.
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Rollback: deploy previous code, all new columns ignored. metadata_json still populated.
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Both options have safe rollback via old code deployment. Option A is slightly safer due to smaller per-deployment surface.

stage_payload.decision_summary: The reception inbox stores 23 metadata fields as a JSON blob in a single `metadata_json TEXT` column. Every access requires `JSON.parse`, only one field (`needsManualDraft`) is queried at the SQL level (via `json_extract`), and 7 route handlers use the spread-merge pattern to update subsets of fields. Five mutating route handlers plus sync and recovery use a spread-merge pattern to update subsets of fields; two additional read-only handlers return parsed metadata to the client. The decision is how to promote these fields to proper columns: all at once, in tiers, or not at all (retaining the bl
goals: Eliminate `JSON.parse` overhead on every metadata access
Enable SQL-level indexing and querying of frequently-filtered fields
Unify the three divergent metadata type definitions into a single canonical type
Maintain API contract stability (both the summary fields and the full `metadata` response object)
non_goals: Normalizing other JSON columns (`payload_json`, `quality_json`, `interpret_json`) on other tables
Redesigning the inbox data model beyond metadata promotion
Adding new queryable features (e.g. search by guest name) -- that is a downstream benefit, not a goal of this migration
constraints: D1 (SQLite) with forward-only sequential SQL migrations
No ORM -- all queries are hand-written prepared statements
Production data exists; migration must be non-destructive
Thread detail API returns the full parsed metadata object to the client (`[threadId]/route.ts` line 78)
`guestRoomNumbers` is an array type (requires TEXT serialization in SQLite)
assumptions: Row count is modest (hundreds to low thousands) -- backfill is trivial
Single-operator system -- brief downtime acceptable if needed

evaluation_criteria.Why it matters: Production data exists; must not lose or corrupt metadata
Thread detail endpoint returns full metadata object; client reads fields directly
7 route handlers + 3 core modules + 13+ test files need updating
D1 migrations are forward-only; rollback = deploy old code
Three divergent types cause maintenance burden and field drift risk
Only one SQL `json_extract` today; modest row count limits performance benefit

options_considered.Option: A: Phased column promotion with tiers
options_considered.Description: Promote fields in 2-3 tiers, dual-write metadata_json + columns during transition, remove blob later
options_considered.Upside: Lowest risk per deployment; can stop at any tier; rollback is trivial
options_considered.Downside: More migrations; dual-write complexity; longer total timeline
options_considered.Key risks: Dual-write logic could diverge; intermediate state harder to test

options_considered.Description: One migration adds all columns, one code change updates all read/write paths, backfill, keep metadata_json populated via dual-write
options_considered.Upside: Simplest mental model; one migration, one code change; type unification happens once
options_considered.Downside: Larger blast radius per deployment; all-or-nothing; harder to review
options_considered.Key risks: Large PR; if any path missed, data loss for that field

options_considered.Option: C: Retain blob, add generated columns
options_considered.Description: Use SQLite generated columns (`AS json_extract(metadata_json, '$.field') STORED`) for query-critical fields only
options_considered.Upside: Zero application code change for read paths; immediate index benefit
options_considered.Downside: Only covers query use case; JSON.parse still needed everywhere; type fragmentation persists; D1 generated column support uncertain
options_considered.Key risks: D1 may not support generated columns; no type safety improvement; does not address the core problem

rejected_approaches: **Option A (Phased)** -- Rejected because phasing does not meaningfully reduce risk when the same 5 mutating handlers + 2 core modules must be updated regardless of tier count. Both options require dual-write for rollback safety; the difference is that phasing adds tier bookkeeping (tracking which fields are columns vs. still blob-only). The modest row count and single-operator deployment model make this incremental safety negligible.

**Option C (Generated columns)** -- Rejected because it does not address the core problem (JSON.parse overhead, type fragmentation, spread-merge complexity) and D1 generated column support is uncertain.

planning_focus: Single migration file (`0006_inbox_metadata_columns.sql`) adding all promoted columns with NULL defaults
Backfill script (SQL UPDATE using `json_extract` per field)
Unified `ThreadMetadata` type replacing all three current definitions
Updated `parseThreadMetadata` that reads from columns (with metadata_json fallback for robustness)
validation_implications: All existing inbox tests must pass with new schema
API response shape must be identical (thread summary fields + full metadata object)
New unit tests for the unified `parseThreadMetadata` column mapper
Migration SQL tested against empty and populated D1 databases
sequencing_constraints: Migration must run before code deployment (D1 migrations applied via `wrangler d1 migrations apply` before deploy)
Backfill must run after migration but can be part of the same migration file
Code changes can deploy after migration is applied (columns exist, old code ignores them)
risks_to_carry_forward: `guestRoomNumbers` is an array; must be stored as JSON TEXT in the column and parsed on read (same as current behavior, just in a named column instead of inside the blob)
`channel` is read-side only; should NOT get a column -- it is computed from other context by `resolveInboxChannelAdapter`
Test fixture volume (13+ files) means the PR will have significant test churn
